coredata.dc:title: Accelerating the extracellular electron transfer of Shewanella oneidensis MR-1 by carbon dots: The role of carbon dots concentration
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Peng
Yang, Chenhui
Li, Zeng
Liu, Jia
Xiao, Xiang
Li, Da
Chen, Chong
Yu, Miao
Feng, Yujie
coredata.dc:description: The high performance of microbial extracellular electron transfer (EET) is essential for bioremediation and bioenergy generation. Carbon dots (CDs) modified electroactive microorganisms showed an increased electron transfer efficiency. However, the influence of CDs’ concentration the EET has not been illustrated before. Herein, we reported that the addition of CDs increased the current generation by accelerating the EET process. The maximum current output of microbial electrochemical cells with the addition of CDs was increased by more than 5 times compared to pure Shewanella oneidensis MR-1. With CDs addition, the increase of biofilm immobilization and flavins secretion improved the direct and mediated EET process. Electrochemical analyses clarified that the combination of cytochrome c with CDs resulted in a faster electron transfer rate. The overload of CDs would restrict their promotion effect on EET, which may be due to their biotoxicity to bacteria. This work offered the insight of CDs’ concentration on the efficiency of EET, not only promoting the related applications of EET but also inspiring the regulation of microorganism function via nanomaterial.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Extracellular electron transfer
Carbon dots
Shewanella oneidensis MR-1

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical reactor setup and bacteria growth
The analysis of riboflavin and quantitative biofilm detection
Enhanced electricity generation with CDs addition
CV analyses of the anode with and without CDs
Enhanced anodic bacteria adhesion and riboflavin production by CDs addition

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Current versus time curves of S. oneidensis MR-1 with different concentration of CDs; (b) Coulombs of charges collected as a function of CDs concentration. Turnover (c) and nonturnover (d) CV curves of the anodes without and with CDs (100 μg mL−1). All scans were conducted at 1 mV s−1.
(a) The bacteria mass in the solution and on the anode; (b) the riboflavin concentration of acolyte with different CDs concentration.
TEM images of the cell without (a, b) and with CDs (c, d); The interaction between CDs and Mtr EET pathway (e).

head.ce:title: Accelerating the extracellular electron transfer of Shewanella oneidensis MR-1 by carbon dots: The role of carbon dots concentration

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The high performance of microbial extracellular electron transfer (EET) is essential for bioremediation and bioenergy generation. Carbon dots (CDs) modified electroactive microorganisms showed an increased electron transfer efficiency. However, the influence of CDs’ concentration the EET has not been illustrated before. Herein, we reported that the addition of CDs increased the current generation by accelerating the EET process. The maximum current output of microbial electrochemical cells with the addition of CDs was increased by more than 5 times compared to pure Shewanella oneidensis MR-1. With CDs addition, the increase of biofilm immobilization and flavins secretion improved the direct and mediated EET process. Electrochemical analyses clarified that the combination of cytochrome c with CDs resulted in a faster electron transfer rate. The overload of CDs would restrict their promotion effect on EET, which may be due to their biotoxicity to bacteria. This work offered the insight of CDs’ concentration on the efficiency of EET, not only promoting the related applications of EET but also inspiring the regulation of microorganism function via nanomaterial.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemically active bacteria (EAB) can transfer electrons produced from the metabolism of organic matters to electron acceptors via extracellular electron transfer (EET), which plays essential roles in the areas of energy production, environmental remediation, and geochemical cycle systems [1–4]. The microbial EET process is vital for biomanufacturing pathways optimization of electricity and has attracted much research attention. EAB can adopt different EET mechanisms for bioelectricity collection, including: (1) mediated electron transfer (MET), through exogenous and/or endogenous diffusive electron transfer mediators; (2) direct electron transfer (DET), through conductive nanowire or cytochromes c on the bacterial outer membrane (OM) [5]. However, the sluggish EET rate from microorganisms to the electrode exhibited unsatisfactorily low power output, limiting the applications of bioelectrochemical systems [6–8].

For extracellular respiring bacteria, such as Geobacter and Shewanella species, cytochrome, a membrane-associated and iron-containing hemeprotein, is vital for EET, since a series of cytochrome were organized into orders according to their reduction potentials and associated with each other to form the electron transport chains throughout the cell membrane [9]. Many efforts have been carried out to boost the EET, including enhancing cytochrome proteins transcription of EAB and regulating gene expression of cytochrome proteins in non-native organisms by synthetic biology [10,11]. However, the complex operation, high cost, and restricted bacterial genus limited the improvement of the electron transport efficiency of cytochromes.

Carbon dots (CDs) are typically quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles comprising amorphous to nanocrystalline carbon cores with surface functional groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl, etc.) [12,13]. Due to their unique structural, electronic, and optical properties, CDs have been reported to permeate into the cell membrane and can be conjugated to bioactive moieties of bacteria or fabricated with adequate functionality conjugation to a biorecognition element, including proteins [14,15]. S. oneidensis MR-1 is one of the well-known microorganisms that is believed to fulfil EET process by both DET and MET process with membrane-bound cytochromes [16,17]. Interestingly, CDs could not only intercalate into the EAB membrane and accelerate the EET rate of cytochromes by their superior conductivity but also increase the metabolic rate of S. oneidensis MR-1 with higher adenosine triphosphate level and more riboflavin production [18]. Similarly, the transmembrane silver nanoparticles could also boost the electron extraction from Shewanella cells [19]. However, the overload of silver nanoparticles or CDs may possess certain biotoxicity to the EET process, leading to lower bioelectricity generation. In this regard, it is urgently needed to examine the influence of CDs’ concentration on the EET of S. oneidensis MR-1.

In this paper, the electrochemical reactors of S. oneidensis MR-1with different CDs` concentration were statistically compared. For each reactor, current output vs.time curves and cyclic voltammetry (CV) were conducted to figure out the extracellular electron transfer respond to CDs. The improvement of immobilized biofilm formation and flavins secretion by CDs boost the direct and intermediate EET process. In addition, the electrochemical performance of cytochromes with different CDs concentrations was also compared with the pure cytochromes to confirm the CDs effect on electron transfer of cytochromes.

The chemicals used in this work were naphthalene (98.0%, Aladdin), sodium (98.0%, Aladdin), ethylene glycol dimethyl ether (99.5%, Aladdin), and ethanediamine (99.5%, Aladdin). Deionized (DI) water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ•cm was obtained from a Milli-Q Water Purification System and used in all the experiments.

The CDs were prepared by one-pot method. Typically, sodium-naphthalene agent were prepared by ultrasound treatment of ethylene glycol dimethyl ether (20 mL) of naphthalene (1.0 g) and, sodium (0.2 g) for 1 h. Ethanediamine (0.2 mL) was added into the aforementioned solution. After 4 h stirring, the product was evaporated by the rotary evaporation apparatus under low pressure, then was re-dispersed in deionized water and centrifuged at 6000 Rad min−1, the supernatant were dialyzed by a dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cut off 3000.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical reactor setup and bacteria growth
ce:section.ce:para: Single chamber electrochemical reactors of 42 mL with 3-electrode configuration were used as previously described (Fig. S1) [20]. The Ag/AgCl electrode (0.198 V vs standard hydrogen electrode) and 0.1 mm Pt wire were used as the reference and counter electrode, respectively. The carbon cloths, with an area of 2 cm2 were used as the working electrode, which was connected to the external circuit by Titanium wire. The electrochemical reactors, which were maintained at 30 °C during the experiment, were connected to an 8-channel potentiostat (CHI600E, China) monitored by a computer.

The wild type S. oneidensis MR-1 (ATCC number is 700550TM) and its mutant strains about metallic respiration (Mtr) pathway were cultured at 30 °C in LB broth aerobically at 200 rpm until the late stationary phase was achieved. The cells were collected by centrifugation (10000 rpm, 3 min) and washed with 50 mM phosphate buffer solution (PBS, Na2HPO4•12H2O, 10.32 g L−1; NaH2PO4•2H2O, 3.32 g L−1; NH4Cl, 0.31 g L−1; KCl, 0.13 g L−1; trace minerals and vitamins) for three times [21]. Then, the cell pellets were re-suspended with electrolyte (50 mM PBS, 18 mM lactate and 1% LB broth) and inoculated into electrochemical reactors chambers at an applied potential of 0.2 V (vs. Ag/AgCl reference electrode).

CV analyses were conducted by an electrochemical workstation (Metrohm, Autolab, B.V.) with Ag/AgCl electrode and Pt wire as the reference electrode and counter electrode, respectively. When the electrochemical reactors had run out of the nutrients, the CVs with scan rate of 1 mV s−1 were done from -0.6 to 0.1 V to get the CV curves at the non-turnover conditions. CV curves with the turnover current were obtained by adding 18 mM sodium lactate after the non-turnover conditions.

The voltammetry behaviours of cytochrome c (from equine heart, Sangon Biotech, Shanghai) with and without CDs were compared on glassy carbon (GC) electrode by this Autolab electrochemical workstation. In this system, the GC electrode and Pt plate electrode (1 × 1cm) were utilized as the working electrode and counter electrode, respectively, with the Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode. Before the CV test, the GC electrode (d=3 mm) was consecutively polished by alumina polishing powder, rinsed with excess water and ethanol, and briefly sonicated before each experiment. 50 mM PBS was used as the electrolyte and extensively purged with nitrogen before experiments. Cytochrome c of 1 mg mL−1 and CDs of 300 μg mL−1 were mixed for 5 min. Then, 6 μL of the mixed solution was dropped on the surface of GC electrode and dried at room temperature before test [22].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The analysis of riboflavin and quantitative biofilm detection
ce:section.ce:para: The concentration of riboflavin was analyzed by using ultra performance liquid chromatography (UPLC) according to the study before [23]. In brief, 2 ml of electrolyte from the electrochemical reactor was centrifuged (10000 rpm, 3 min) and the supernatant was filtrated with 0.22 μm cellulose membrane and then subjected to UPLC system for analysis. Riboflavin was separated in a solvent system of water-methanol-acetic acid (68: 32: 0.1 v/v) and a flow rate of 0.1 mL min−1, with the UPLC profile monitored at 254 nm.

The concentrations of the protein in the solution and on the anode were determined by BCA method. Quantitative biofilm formation analyze was conducted based on the protein mass on the surface on carbon cloth anode [24]. Briefly, after the experiment, the carbon cloth anode with certain area was cut down and soaked in 0.1 M KOH solution and heated for half an hour at 90 °C.

The size and composition of the CDs sample were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G20, FEI Co.), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi, Thermo Fisher Scientific, using an Al Kα X-ray source) and fourier infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Thermo Fisher Scientific). The absorption and photoluminescence spectra were collected by using an ultraviolet-visible (UV—vis) spectrophotometer (Evolution 300, Thermo Scientific) and fluorescence spectrophotometer (Fluoromax-4, Horiba Jobin Yvon).

For scanning electron microscopy (SEM) test of S. oneidensis MR-1 coated carbon cloth electrodes were removed from the reactors after the operation with/without the addition of CDs. The sample was fixed with 2.5% glutaraldehyde for 12 hours and washed with 50 mM PBS three times and then dehydrated with ethanol gradient (beginning with 50%, 70%, 90% and ending with 100%) in steps of 10 minutes duration. Then, the samples were washed with the mixture of ethanol and tert-butanol (1:1) and pure tert-butanol. Then, the samples were dried with N2, sputter coated with gold, then observed with a field emission scanning electron microscope (FEI XL30 Sirion SEM). The S. oneidensis MR-1 cells with and without CDs were stained by sodium phosphotungstate and observed by TEM (Tecnai G20, FEI Co.).

The morphology of CDs was confirmed by TEM images (Fig.S2a), showing that CDs had a small average diameter of ∼ 2.2 nm. The high-resolution TEM images of a CD (Fig.S2b) showed a lattice spacing of about 0.21 nm, corresponding to the in-plane spacing of graphene (100). The chemical composition of CDs was analyzed by XPS and FTIR spectroscopy. In the FTIR analysis of CDs (Fig. S2c), several characteristic peaks were identified, with that at 3430 cm−1 corresponding to C-OH stretching vibration, those at 2923 cm−1 and 2850 cm−1 corresponding to the C-H stretching vibration, those at 1642 cm−1 and 1465 cm−1 corresponding to C=O stretching and C-H bending vibrations. The XPS measurement was performed to determine the elemental composition of the CDs. The result of the XPS survey shown in Fig. S3 indicated that the CDs were mainly composed of C and O species. From Fig. S2d, the high-resolution C 1s spectrum can be deconvoluted into three sub-peaks: C=C/C-C bond, C-O/C=O bond, and O-C=O bond at 284.6, 285.9 and 289.0 eV, respectively. The high-resolution O 1s spectrum (Fig. S2e) revealed two sub-peaks at 530.7 and 535.1 eV, attributed to C=O/C-O and O-C=O. The result of XPS analysis was in accordance with the FTIR result. Both results indicated that CDs had abundant phenolic hydroxyl groups on their surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced electricity generation with CDs addition
ce:section.ce:para: The microbial EET output was monitored by measuring the extracellular current using a three-electrode system. Lactate (18 mM) was used as the sole electron donor and carbon source for cell respiration. After the inoculation, CDs were immediately added into the reactor at a concentration of 0, 50, 100, 200, and 300 μg mL−1, respectively. Fig. 1 a displays the average current output results as a function of time for the control and test reactors with different CDs concentrations. Moreover, the results showed that all the electrochemical reactors produced apparent current generation after about 2 hours, and the currents increased gradually, with the peak currents achieved at about 15 hours. It was observed that the electrochemical reactors with CDs performed significantly higher current generation than unmodified ones. Specifically, with the CDs concentration of 50 μg mL−1, the corresponding reactor exhibited a peak current of 12.21 μA. While, the reactor with CDs concentration of 100 μg mL−1 achieved a maximum peak current of 39.48 μA, which was more than 5 times higher than the control one (7.70 μA). However, when the CDs concentration increased to 200 and 300 μg mL−1, the peak currents showed a slightly decrease. The peak currents of reactors with CDs concentration of 200 and 300 μg mL−1 were 36.94 and 32.74 μA, which were still 4.80 and 4.25 times higher than the control one, respectively. While no apparent electrochemical signal of CDs was observed in Fig. S4. The number of coulombs of electrons collected by each electrochemical reactor at the end of experiment was calculated by integrating the area under individual CA curves and the values were plotted in Fig. 1b. CD (100 μg mL−1) added S. oneidensis MR-1 reactors were able to harvest up to nearly 3 times more electrons (2.20 C at 100 μg mL−1) in comparison with the pure S. oneidensis MR-1 control one (0.55 C). Thus, it could be concluded that the addition of CDs in the anode system could greatly improve the electricity generation ability of S. oneidensis MR-1; however, superfluous CDs addition could weaken the enhancement effect of CDs on electricity generation, which may due to the inhibition effect of CDs on bacteria propagation [25].

ce:section.ce:section-title: CV analyses of the anode with and without CDs
ce:section.ce:para: CV analyses were further carried out to explore the mechanism for the improved electricity generation performance of S. oneidensis MR-1. The turnover CV measurements without and with CDs of 100 μg mL−1 in Fig. 1c revealed three pairs of primary reversible catalytic electron transfer waves as the potential was swept past E= -0.42 V, -0.25 V, and 0.05 V, where the current response rises rapidly [20,26]. No local maxima in the current response were observed, indicating the lactate mass transport is not a limitation. A similar phenomenon was also observed for the anode with other CDs concentrations in Fig. S5, and the variation of the CV respond current as CDs concentration showed the same trend as the electricity generation in Fig. 1a. It is obvious that the current output at CV potential (E= +0.10 V) in Fig. S5 is similar in amplitude to the current at the last time points in Fig. 1a, indicating that the electrochemical analyses accurately interrogate the respiring biofilms [22].

Fig. 1d showed the CV curves of anodes at the scanning rate of 1 mV s−1 upon the depletion of nutrients. For both curves, three pairs of peaks showed up at the same potential recorded in turnover CV curves. The results were concluded that the peaks centered at -0.42 V were associated with the self-synthesized riboflavin [27–29], indicating the MET processes. The pair of peaks centered at 0.05 V and -0.20 V corresponded to the OM cytochrome c that controlled the DET process [30,31]. It was observed that the peak currents of three pairs of peaks of the anodes with CDs were all higher than that of the control one in both turnover and nonturnover conditions. The peaks associated with riboflavin for the anode with CDs have a much higher responding current than that without CDs, indicating that CDs improved the MET process, implying that CDs might induce the overproduction of riboflavin by S. oneidensis MR-1 [23]. Meanwhile, the peaks associated with cytochrome c for the carbon cloth anode with CDs also showed a much higher currents response than the control one, meaning that higher activity or much more content of cytochrome c was detected. This enhancement effect of the DET process might be due to more bacteria attached to the anode or higher electrochemical activity of cytochrome on the OM of S. oneidensis MR-1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced anodic bacteria adhesion and riboflavin production by CDs addition
ce:section.ce:para: The results of bacteria biomass in the solution and on the anode were analyzed (Fig. 2 a), and the result showed that bacteria biomass concentration in the solution decreased as the CDs concentration increased, which may be due to certain inhibition effect of CDs on bacteria propagation [25]. Without CDs, the biomass concentration in the solution could reach to more than 51.70 mg L−1. When the concentration of CDs increased to 300 μg mL−1, the biomass concentration decreased to about 35.70 mg L−1, which was only about 70% of the control one. However, the variation of biomass density on the anodes did not show the same trend as that in the solution. The biomass density reached a maximum value (56.4 μg cm−2) when the CDs concentration was 100 μg mL−1. In contrast, the biomass density of the control one was just 21.40 μg cm−2. When the CDs concentration increased to 200 and 300 μg mL−1, the biomass density on the anode decreased to 29.70 and 26.90 μg cm−2, respectively, just slightly higher than that of the control one. The results indicated that appropriate CDs addition could enhance the S. oneidensis MR-1 colonization on the carbon cloth anode. The reduction of biomass density on anode with CDs concentration of 200 and 300 μg mL−1 may be due to the inhibition effect of CDs on the propagation of S. oneidensis MR-1. The variation trend of biomass density on anode with CDs concentration was the same as that of electricity generation and CV curves, indicating that the enhanced DET process may partly due to more S. oneidensis MR-1 colonization on the anode, which means that more cytochrome c existed on the surface of the anode. SEM images of the anode were shown in Fig. S6, S. oneidensis MR-1 bacteria colonization on the anode with CDs concentration of 100 μg mL−1 reached a maximum condition, consistent with the aforementioned biomass analyses as mentioned earlier.

The concentration of riboflavin was detected to analyze the effect of CDs addition on the MET process. As shown in Fig. 2b, with the addition of CDs (100 μg mL−1), the riboflavin concentration reached a peak value of 1.10 μg mL−1, which was 5.5 folds of the control one (0.20 μg mL−1). When the CDs concentration increased to 200 μg mL−1, the riboflavin concentration decreased slightly to 1.00 μg mL−1, which is obviously higher than the control one (0.20 μg mL−1) as well. The riboflavin secretion with more CDs addition (300 μg mL−1) was reduced but still higher than the control one (0.40 μg mL−1 vs. 0.20 μg mL−1), which may be due to the over addition of CDs. This result confirmed that CDs could induce the overproduction of riboflavin by S. oneidensis MR-1, meaning an enhanced MET process. Melissa et al. once noted that extracellular riboflavin increased with TiO2 nanoparticles addition in S. oneidensis system, which may be an indication that S. oneidensis flavin secretion is activated as a response to a system stressor by altered gene expression.[32] The enhanced riboflavin secretion by CDs addition in this system may be due to a similar mechanism as that of TiO2 nanoparticles.

In conclusion, DET and MET processes could be enhanced by CDs’ addition, resulting in higher electricity generation. Interestingly, the reactors with CDs concentration of 300 μg mL−1 showed almost similar riboflavin concentration and anode biomass density as the control one. However, the peak current with CDs concentration of 300 μg mL−1 (32.74 μA) was still much higher than the control one (7.70 μA). Besides the current output by the MET process, the current generation ability per μg bacteria (just on the anode) was also calculated. For the control reactor, the cells on the anode just generated 0.20 μA per μg bacteria, while 0.60 μA per μg bacteria was obtained with a CDs concentration of 300 μg mL−1, almost 3 times higher than the former. This phenomenon pointed out that the enhanced electricity generation may also be due to EET ability improvement of S. oneidensis MR-1 per cell on the anode. Thus, considering the specific properties of CDs, it could be inferred that CDs can incorporate cytochromes on OM and accelerate the electron transfer rate for EET.

The Mtr respiratory pathway of S. oneidensis MR-1, composed of CymA, MtrA, MtrB, and MtrC/OmcA, is an important transmembrane electron transfer protein channel for the EET process. S. oneidensis MR-1 could utilize the electron acceptor outside of cells for anaerobic propagation through this protein channel pathway. Therefore, it is interesting to know whether the Mtr pathway plays a role in the CDs enhancement effect on S. oneidensis MR-1 electricity generation ability.

Fig. S7a showed that the photoluminescenece (PL) intensity of CDs had a significant decrease after interaction with cytochrome c. It was the aggregation induced by cytochrome c that led to the obvious PL quenching of CDs. The conformational variation of cytochrome c with CDs is provided by UV-vis absorption spectra information. Both the Soret band (408 nm) and the Q-band (529 nm), which are characteristic of native cytochrome c, are not changed with CDs interaction (Fig. S7b), confirming the structure intactness of cytochrome c [22].

The TEM images of the S. oneidensis MR-1 cells with and without CDs` addition were shown in Fig. 3 . For the cell with CDs, lots of “dots” were found on the surface of the cell (Fig. 3d and e); while, without CDs addition, these “dots” were not found on the surface of the pure cell (Fig. 3a and b). This result indicated that the added CDs could be absorbed on the OM, where cytochromes were located. And the interaction between CDs and cytochromes on the OM could accelerate the EET rate through the OM to the anode surface (Fig. 3f). The mechanism of enhanced extracellular electron transfer ability of S. oneidensis MR-1 by adding CDs was proposed that CDs could combine with cytochrome c proteins, which would accelerate the electron transfer rate, and make it easier to transfer the electron from the out-membrane protein to anode surface.

In summary, the addition of CDs to the microbial electrochemical reactor with S. oneidensis MR-1 causes a more than 5-folds current output increase, with a >5-folds increase in charge collection and a 37.50% increase in electrode colonization. CDs with oxygen-containing groups on their surface not only increase the packing density of the electron transfer chain but also enhance adhesion of the CD-bacteria system on the anode, which further promoted the DET. The CDs` addition with the proper amount could also stimulate the secretion of flavin, which enhances the MET rate as well. Thus, the EET of S. oneidensis MR-1 was greatly enhanced by a synergistic effect. However, the overdose of CDs restricts the promotion effect on EET performance of S. oneidensis MR-1 due to the biotoxicity. This facile enhancement method of S. oneidensis MR-1 by the CDs` addition holds great potential to promote the application of EET and benefit the understanding about the function of nanomaterials in the EET process.

Peng Zhang: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Chenhui Yang: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Zeng Li: Writing – original draft. Jia Liu: Writing – original draft. Xiang Xiao: Writing – review & editing. Da Li: Writing – review & editing. Chong Chen: Writing – review & editing. Miao Yu: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Yujie Feng: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Fund of China (Grand No. 41907278). The authors also acknowledged the Yunnan Fundamental Research Projects (grant NO. 202101AW070009 and 202001AU070049) and the Undergraduate Training Programs for Innovation and Entrepreneurship (20211067400159). This work was also supported by the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2021M690572).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2022.140490.
